channel.title: Science
channel.description: The very best in news and research across the sciences.

item.title: Exclusive: Death of girl in Chinese gene-editing trial was never made public
item.description: Parents want accountability after study went disastrously wrong, Science and Retraction Watch investigation reveals

item.title: What lies beyond the Fields Medal? | Science
item.description: The International Congress of Mathematicians (ICM), a quadrennial conference dating back to the 19th century, kicks off today in Philadelphia, PA, USA. The highlight of the opening ceremony is the announcement of a new cohort of Fields medalists. The award is often described as the Nobel Prize of mathematics, but Fields medalists are conspicuously younger than Nobel Laureates, who typically conduct their award-winning research in chemistry, economics, medicine, or physics in their mid-40s and receive the award decades later. Mathematicians are only eligible for the Fields Medal until the year they turn 40. Why is mathematics' highest honor reserved exclusively for the young?

item.title: Single-cell multiomics connects 3D genome and transcriptome alterations in Alzheimer’s disease | Science
item.description: Alzheimer’s disease (AD) disrupts brain function through cell type–specific transcriptomic and epigenomic alterations, yet the contribution of three-dimensional (3D) genome organization to AD remains poorly understood. We applied GAGE-seq (genome ...

item.title: Single-cell multiomics and chromatin structure reveal gene-regulatory dynamics in heart failure | Science
item.description: Heart failure is a leading cause of morbidity and mortality, yet gene-regulatory mechanisms driving cell type–specific pathologic responses remain undefined. Here, we present the cell type–resolved transcriptomes, chromatin accessibility, histone ...

item.title: Human body single-cell atlas of three-dimensional genome organization and DNA methylation | Science
item.description: Higher-order chromatin structure and DNA methylation are critical for gene regulation, but how these vary across the human body remains unclear. We performed multiomic profiling of three-dimensional (3D) genome structure and DNA methylation for 86,689 ...

item.title: A 3D genome atlas of human tonsil and the role of loop extrusion in B cell somatic hypermutation | Science
item.description: B cell maturation within the germinal center tissue microenvironment involves immunoglobulin gene diversification by somatic hypermutation (SHM). How three-dimensional (3D) genome architecture influences SHM is not fully understood. We leveraged ...

item.title: Dose-dependent sensitivity of human three-dimensional chromatin to a heart disease–linked transcription factor | Science
item.description: Dosage-sensitive transcription factors (TFs) underlie altered gene regulation in human developmental disorders, and cell type–specific gene regulation is linked to the reorganization of three-dimensional (3D) chromatin during cellular differentiation. In ...

item.title: Epigenetic and 3D genome reprogramming during the aging of the human hippocampus | Science
item.description: Changes in gene expression have been observed in the aging human brain, but our understanding of the underlying regulatory mechanisms remains limited. To unravel these complexities, we analyzed single-nucleus gene expression, chromatin accessibility, DNA ...

item.title: The global biogeography of passerine songs | Science
item.description: Although bird songs are classic models for understanding the evolution of vocal communication, their global diversity has long made the development of a unifying framework challenging. By analyzing the acoustic architecture of songs from more than 3000 ...

item.title: Invasive species’ environmental impacts are more severe in the Global South | Science
item.description: Invasive alien species (IAS) are a major threat to biodiversity, yet their impact distribution remains poorly understood. Global biodiversity assessments suggest the highest impacts in wealthy countries of the Global North, but this is only inferred from ...

item.title: In Other Journals | Science
item.description: Editors’ selections from the current scientific literature

item.title: In Science Journals | Science
item.description: Highlights from the Science family of journals

item.title: Genome in motion: Mapping the nucleus in health | Science
item.description: HomeScienceVol. 393, No. 6809Genome in motion: Mapping the nucleus in healthBack To Vol. 393, No. 6809 Full accessIntroduction to Special IssueINTRODUCTION Share on Genome in motion: Mapping the nucleus in healthDi Jiang, Yevgeniya Nusinovich, and Sarah RossAuthors Info & AffiliationsScience23 Jul 2026Vol 393, Issue 6809pp. 364-365DOI: 10.1126/science.aek3868 NEXT ARTICLEUniversity science in the…

item.title: Protect and expand ocean observation systems | Science
item.description: The United States alone is responsible for roughly half of the world’s ocean monitoring platforms (1). In May, the US government announced plans to decommission and remove more than 900 deep-sea instruments moored across the Atlantic and Pacific oceans (2). Although implementation has since been paused (3), the episode exposed the vulnerability of a global ocean observation system that is essential to global risk management infrastructure. The international community must take urgent steps to broaden the coalition of countries investing in ocean observation infrastructure.

item.title: Seafood biosecurity gaps threaten biodiversity | Science
item.description: Biological invasions pose serious threats to biodiversity, ecosystem function, economies, and human health (1). Live seafood trading is a major pathway for the introduction of non-native and invasive species (2–5). Although existing local and global frameworks align with international treaties, regulations are often outdated, weakly enforced, and inconsistent across countries (6–9). To address these gaps, a more robust and coordinated approach is needed to reinforce existing biosecurity protocols.

item.title: Brazil’s environmental governance at risk | Science

item.title: Dispatches from the front lines of science | Science
item.description: Young scientists’ passion and commitment take center stage in a moving portrait of research in action

item.title: The tangled world of snake taxonomy | Science
item.description: Fringe figures in herpetology advance unlikely (and unchecked) claims in an offbeat taxonomical history

item.title: A tiny cone of reactivity | Science
item.description: Colliding molecules react only when their orientations and point of impact fall within a narrow range

item.title: No strain, no gain | Science
item.description: A modular approach could expand the repertoire of spring-loaded covalent drugs

item.title: Ballooning under water | Science
item.description: Insect larval buoyancy is controlled by material properties of their air sacs

item.title: Birdsong diversity across the world | Science
item.description: Birdsong motifs are shaped by evolutionary history, biological traits, and environmental constraints

item.title: University science in the US needs a coherent plan | Science
item.description: The Trump administration continues to degrade America’s scientific establishment, causing widespread harm to the country’s broader interests and well-being. Last week, the Association of American Universities reported that graduate student enrollment in ...

item.title: Contagious fish cancer overruns New England lake
item.description: Catfish melanomas are the first example of a transmissible cancer in fish, genetic studies reveal

item.title: Rapid spread of Ebola spurs ‘remarkably’ quick drug and vaccine trials
item.description: Pioneering studies are underway to address massive outbreak of rare Bundibugyo virus in the Congo

item.title: China’s detention of U.S. seismologist and data-sharing crackdown alarm researchers
item.description: Youlin Chen analyzed earthquake data that can also aid nuclear-test monitoring

item.title: Prominent Salk Institute biologist to resign following sexual misconduct investigation
item.description: Staffers complain that Salk allowed circadian rhythms researcher Satchidananda Panda to depart quietly, while the allegations and findings remain under wraps

item.title: Alzheimer’s trial results lend momentum to strategies targeting tau
item.description: First drug to lower the protein and slow cognitive decline creates buzz despite puzzling data

item.title: Oldest known Mars rock offers glimpse of planet’s watery youth
item.description: Ancient Teghaza meteorite hints at granitelike crust and shows Mars was already losing its water 4.1 billion years ago

item.title: Climate scientists sharpen tools for linking global warming to extreme weather
item.description: National Academies report says the fast-growing field can boost confidence with more rigorous results
